question: What is the difference between let and var in Swift?
answer:
• let — immutable binding. The reference (or value) cannot be reassigned after initialization.
• var — mutable binding. The reference (or value) can be reassigned.

Swift defaults to let in idiomatic code — the compiler will warn when a var is never mutated. For value types (structs, enums) let also makes the inner properties immutable. For reference types (classes) let only locks the reference; the object's mutable properties are still settable.

question: What are optionals in Swift and how do you safely unwrap them?
answer:
An Optional<T> is an enum with two cases: .some(T) or .none — Swift's encoding of "value may be missing". Written as T?.

Safe unwrapping options:
• if let / guard let — bind only if non-nil
• ?? — nil-coalescing default
• optional chaining ?. — propagates nil
• map / flatMap — transform without unwrapping

Force-unwrap with ! crashes on nil — use only when truly unreachable.

question: Explain value types vs reference types in Swift.
answer:
Swift has two kinds of types:

• Value types — struct, enum, tuple. Copied on assignment / argument passing. Each variable owns its copy. Thread-safer by default.
• Reference types — class, closure, actor. Shared by reference. Multiple variables can point at the same instance — mutation is visible to all.

Apple guidance: prefer struct unless you need reference semantics (identity, inheritance, deinit, NSObject bridging). Standard library collections (Array, Dictionary, Set, String) are all value types with copy-on-write under the hood.

question: What are closures in Swift and how do capture lists work?
answer:
Closures are self-contained blocks of functionality (named or anonymous) that can be passed around. They capture variables from their surrounding scope by reference (for reference types) or by copying (for value types).

A capture list `[weak self]` / `[unowned self]` lets you opt into different memory semantics — required to avoid retain cycles when a closure outlives its owner (e.g. stored on a view, networking layer, or NotificationCenter).

Trailing-closure syntax + shorthand parameters ($0, $1) make Swift closures concise.

question: What is the difference between Array, Set, and Dictionary in Swift?
answer:
Three core collections from the standard library — all value types, generic, and copy-on-write:

• Array<T> — ordered, indexed, allows duplicates. O(1) random access, O(1) amortized append.
• Set<T> — unordered, unique elements (T must be Hashable). O(1) average insert/contains.
• Dictionary<K, V> — unordered key/value pairs (K must be Hashable). Unique keys.

All three integrate with Sequence/Collection protocols, so map/filter/reduce/forEach/sort work uniformly.

question: Explain Swift error handling: throws, try, do/catch, Result.
answer:
Swift uses checked exceptions: a function that may fail is marked `throws` and the caller must use `try`. Errors are values conforming to the Error protocol (typically enums).

Forms of try:
• try — propagates the error up
• try? — converts to Optional (nil on error)
• try! — force, traps on error

do/catch handles errors locally. For async returning either success or failure as data, use Result<Success, Failure>.

question: What are tuples, ranges, and the guard statement in Swift?
answer:
Three small but high-impact features:

• Tuple — fixed-size group of typed values, optionally named. Useful for ad-hoc returns without declaring a struct.
• Range / ClosedRange — half-open `0..<10` and closed `1...10`. Conform to Sequence so you can iterate, slice strings/arrays.
• guard — early-exit statement that requires you to leave the current scope on failure. Keeps the happy path unindented and forces unwrapping into the rest of the function.
